Article.ArticleTitle: Exclusions affect representativeness of Alzheimer's disease trial participants.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study assessed how medical exclusion criteria of a prevention Alzheimer's disease trial impact potential eligibility and sample characteristics.

Medical exclusion criteria from the Anti-Amyloid Treatment in Asymptomatic Alzheimer's Disease (A4) trial were applied to N = 3695 participants from the population-based Health Retirement Study (HRS) with linked Medicare data. We determined the proportion of hypothetical eligibility. Multivariate Poisson regression was used to estimate associations between sociodemographic characteristics and eligibility.

Of the participants, 74.2% (N = 2742) were deemed ineligible. Accounting for all sociodemographic characteristics, older age, female gender, fewer years of education, lower net worth, and body mass index of 30+ was associated with a higher odds of being deemed ineligible. Ethnocultural identity and living arrangement were not associated with eligibility.

Our results suggest that certain sociodemographic factors may limit eligibility for a prevention Alzheimer's disease trial due to the presence of exclusionary medical conditions. This highlights the need to make trials more inclusive.

A total of 2742 participants (74.2%) were deemed ineligible for the Anti-Amyloid Treatment in Asymptomatic Alzheimer's Disease (A4) trial based on the medical exclusion criteria.The most common medical exclusion in this sample was for cardiovascular conditions.Ineligibility was associated with age, education, net worth, and body mass index.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Miriam T. Ashford reports grants from the National Institute on Aging (NIA; made to the institution) and declares no potential conflicts of interest. Rachana Tank and Mohammed U. Kabeto declare no potential conflicts of interest. Rachel L. Nosheny reports grants from the National Institutes of Health (NIH), grants from Genentech, Inc., and grants from the California Department of Public Health during the conduct of the study. Michael W. Weiner reports grants from the NIH, from the Department of Defense (DOD), from the Patient‐Centered Outcomes Research Institute (PCORI), from California Department of Public Health (CDPH), from the University of Michigan, from Siemens, from Biogen, from Hillblom Foundation, from the Alzheimer's Association, from the State of California, from Johnson & Johnson, from Kevin and Connie Shanahan, from general electric (GE), from vanderbilt university medical center (VUmc), from Australian Catholic University (HBI‐BHR), from The Stroke Foundation, and from the Veterans Administration; personal fees from Acumen Pharmaceutical, from Cerecin, from Dolby Family Ventures, from Eli Lilly, from Merck Sharp & Dohme Corp., from the National Institute on Aging (NIA), from Nestle/Nestec, from PCORI/patient‐powered research network (PPRN), from Roche, from the University of Southern California (USC), from NervGen, from Baird Equity Capital, from BioClinica, from Cytox, from Duke University, personal fees from Eisai, personal fees from FUJIFILM‐Toyama Chemical (Japan), personal fees from Garfield Weston, from Genentech, from Guidepoint Global, from Indiana University, from Japanese Organization for Medical Device Development, Inc. (JOMDD), from Medscape, from Peerview Internal Medicine, rom Roche, from T3D Therapeutics, from WebMD, from Vida Ventures, from The Buck Institute for Research on Aging, from China Association for Alzheimer's Disease (CAAD), from the Japan Society for Dementia Research, and from the Korean Dementia Society, outside the submitted work; and holds stocks or options with Alzheon Inc., Alzeca, and Anven. David R. Weir receives grant from the NIA.National Institute on Aging (U01 AG009740) for the Health and Retirement Study (HRS). Kenneth M. Langa receives grants from the NIH and the Alzheimer's Association; consulting fees from NIH‐funded projects at Harvard, the University of Pennsylvania, the University of Minnesota, the University of Colorado, and the University of Southern California; expert testimony related to decisional capacity in a person with dementia; and participation on a Data Safety Monitoring Board for a clinical trial at Indiana University. Any author disclosures are available in the Supporting Information.
